sentences.text: Obviously, he didn't have a job or anything to go to.
I think he was still trading some while.
I don't-- I don't know exactly how.

That was one of the-- thinking about that question-- you know, my brother, when he came back from the war into the ghetto, he-- on one of my brothers tapes-- because they had a big feast and everything.
It was very good food and everything.
And so he asked my father, well, how do you make a living?
And the classic response is, we get-- we get by, you know, whatever that means.
I don't know.
I wish they were able to fill a few details of what getting by meant.
But I don't-- I don't know.
I suspect-- I suspect that my brother was able to collect some business debts from the Poles, from some of his Polish business associates.
And that helped a lot.
The town was full of smuggling.
Smuggling was the major industry there.
And I think they tried to get in on it, too.
And I remember one incident where they gave a lot of money to somebody that went out of town, you know, into the Gentile area to buy sugar.
And he-- eventually he came back.
And he said he was robbed and the money was gone.
And of course, they wouldn't believe that he was robbed.
And it was just-- just terrible stuff going on there.
I think they went to the rabbi and they lit black candles, whatever that signified, you know, just that he would swear-- I know they were hysterical about the loss.
Apparently, it was like, a lot of money that they-- a lot of stuff, the resources that they lost on that.
No, that was my-- my father.
Yeah.
Other than that, I-- there was-- he was not a tradesman.
Other people were shoemakers, or tailors, or glaziers, or carpenters.
But my father didn't have any of those skills.
He was a businessman.
So I'm not sure.
But we didn't seem to really-- in the ghetto, we seem to eat adequately.
We were never really hungry.
Yes, I did.
I felt a-- because things were very uncertain.
And there was like, this atmosphere of fear that nobody knew what was going to happen.
Yeah.
I did not witness it myself.
But I know that there was violence.
There were shootings in the synagogue, the major synagogue.
And the great synagogue in Piotrkow was destroyed.
But at that time, I did not witness any violence myself.
Well, that was-- I did-- I was a part of some violence.
During that period of time, people were recruited to go to work.
There were two major industries in Piotrkow.
One was the glass-working firms.
The two of them.
And one was [?
Bugoi, ?] the woodworking factory.
And I remember that I kind of wanted to be with the big boys.
So I kind of volunteered to go and work at the glass factory.
And I remember walking with a bunch of us, where we would walk out of the ghetto, and we would walk to this glass factory.
It was a mile or so away.
And I would-- I worked there.
And what I would do is that they were blowing glass by hand.
And the way that it looked was like-- was like a furnace.
It was up high.
And it was like gradual platforms.

And the master, who was on top, that was in charge of a crew, would put this big stick into the glass furnace, pick up a little bit of glass, and they would blow it like that, by hand, with the mouth, they would blow it, hand it down to the other person.
And we would slowly roll it and detach it from that stick.
And then it was my job to pick it up, while it was still red hot, and run it to a cool-off conveyor belt, where the glass got usually cool.
And you had to be pretty fast because if you spent too much time getting it to the conveyor belt, the glass would break.
And if it did, that Pollock would give you a good hit over the head-- not often, but sometimes, you know.
But I kind of liked it.
It was very exciting for me, as a kid, to be-- you know, to be there-- probably no more than 10 years old.
And it was kind of exciting.
And I-- you know, and we got better food because we were there.
And we saw there was a lot of other Polish people that worked there.
And I saw how the glass was made.
And-- and I don't know.
Then, after a time, I didn't work there anymore.
I don't know why.
But I probably stopped going.
I was-- again, I was with a bunch of people that were-- young people that were doing work.
And one day we were unloading a wagon, a lorry, you know, a truck.
And there-- I think there were sacks.
There were flour sacks or something like that.
And I was very proud of myself.
I was able to take one of the flour sacks.
And I was able to carry it and put it down.
But then a guy, a big guy, on top of the truck-- he threw that sack at me, and on my back.
And it hit my back and it hurt me.
And I was really angry.
This son of a bitch didn't know who I was.
You know, I was an important-- son of an important person in that town.
He did something like that to me.
So I went up to this German-- it was a German supervising officer there.
You remember his name?
No, I have no idea.
And I told him that this work was too hard for me, that I didn't want to do it anymore and I got hurt.
So I don't remember him saying anything at all.
He just opened up his holster, took out his revolver, and told me to stand what I thought was like a well.
It was on a brick well.
And he pointed his gun at me and he was going to shoot me.
And everything stopped.
And all the other people around and everybody was stopped, looking at us.
And about at that point, some senior person in the Jewish community I think suddenly came in and started talking to the guy.
And I remember him talking to him very quietly, peacefully, and reasonably, like into his ear.
And after a while, the man took his revolver and put it away.
And so that was my first brush with this.
But I had-- you know, I must say, I've been questioning that.
And I had really no fear, because I had no conception of death.
You know, to me, that was just a play thing.
But I never volunteered after that for any more work.
I didn't do any more work after that.
Well also, the other thing, the tragic-- the very tragic thing that happened during about that time, probably around '41, was that my sister Ronia was pregnant.
And she was going to deliver a baby.
Yeah, she lived in the ghetto with her husband.
And the story goes that my father felt that it would be-- we were not allowed to go outside the ghetto for any health care or anything like that.
You had to get it inside.
There was a makeshift hospital inside the ghetto.
And so he thought it would be much safer for her if she went outside the ghetto to a Polish Catholic hospital, because she didn't look Jewish anyway.
And I think he got help there, from a family there, from the Polish side.
There's a family called Novak that my father did business with before the war, who were very, very nice people.
I remember them quite well.
And I think maybe they helped him arrange for her to get into that hospital.
Well, what happened was either the Polish workers in the hospital told-- must have-- told the Gestapo about it.
The Gestapo came in.
It was like two women that gave birth to children.
She gave birth to a little boy.
And it was like, three, four days later on.
And according to this lady, they came in, they picked up the baby, threw it out the window.
Yeah.
And they took her and they shot her and the other woman as well.
So that was a very-- but I didn't know it at the time, you know, because they wouldn't tell me that.
You know, they kept secrets.
I don't remember.
I just don't remember.
It probably was in '40-- '40, '41.
I would say probably more likely in '41.
Although--
She had no other children.
And it's kind of surprising.
She'd been already married by then maybe eight years or so.
And she had no other children.
This was their first child.
Yeah, yeah, they knew what happened.
And that was confirmed by this lady.
She went-- this Polish lady, this Mrs.
Novak, went to bring something to the hospital for her.
And that's how she found out.
And that's how she told my parents about it.
The major-- major event is I know there was a lot of fear going on.
Because by that time, there was lots of stories coming in about the destruction of the Jewish community, the deportation.
That was the most fearful word, was deportation.

And everybody in the ghetto tried to have some kind of a useful occupation so they would be useful to the German war effort so they-- then they would get a special work permit, work card, stamped with the official German stamp.
And that was the ticket to one's life.
And there was-- actually, you know, in our town, there was a lot of tradespeople that did contribute a lot to the-- to the German war effort.
Shoemakers-- I don't think there was a Polish tailor in our town.
I mean, all the tailoring industry and everything was sent to the-- among Jews-- and shoemakers and things like that.
There were some very essential services that could only be provided by the people in the ghetto.
And the Germans came in.
And they had this uniforms made, and shoes made, and boots, and things like that.
But at that time, my brother tells me the story that he was also concerned about getting some kind of a work permit.
So he went to a friend of his that used to-- he used to do business with.
And don't ask me his name.
I don't know.
And he had a little barrel-making factory.
A barrel-making factory was, you know, making wooden barrels, like you put pickles in, and herring, and things like that.
And it was a small place.
Maybe he had 20, 30 people working for him.
There was two brothers, I think I remember, and a father that had that business.
And my father used to sell them.
Timber before the war.
And he tol-- my brother told this man.
He said, look, why don't you apply to the German authorities and get some permits so you can have Jews work for you?
So he did.
He applied.
And he got permission for 12 Jews to work there.

But my brother also tells me this story that before, he would get one of-- be one of the ones to come to work at that factory, they had to pay him off as well, so not only was he going to get free labor, but he was-- he also demanded some ransom money from us.
OK?
And the major-- the major event in the ghetto was the fear of deportation.
Because the communities just close to us were being deported.
So I remember that almost everybody had some kind of a hiding place in their house, whether it was in the ceiling, or it was in the cellar, false walls, floors.
I think we had something with a-- with a floor, just like in Everybody had a hiding place.
And sometimes in around I think 1942, towards the end of '42, the Germans began what was called the Action.
And what happened was that the Germans and their friends, the Ukrainians, surrounded the ghetto.
And they forced people from their homes onto this assembly place.
And then, at that place, they made their selections.
Well, here's what I remember.
I don't have a perfect recollection at all.
But I'm trying not to go into the history books, but now go back into my memory and remember what I remember.
And I remember that we were standing in this assembly place.
And there was my father, and my brother, and I.
OK, I'll tell you that story in just a minute.
Let me--
We were standing there.
And the people, the Germans, whatever, came in to make the selection--
Yeah, I think so.
And they told me-- and I was at that time, '41, 11 years old.
But I was a small guy.
I was kind of small for my age.
And they told me to stand behind them.
And they-- my father  and my brother stood shoulder to shoulder.
And they told me to stay behind them.
They hid me with their body.
And they told me to be absolutely still, which wasn't easy for me to do.
And when this man came over, they had their work permits.
And I don't know.
Maybe some money even passed hands.
I don't know.
But we were going to the right.
And to the right, because we had the work permits, was the place to live.
Now at the same time, and I don't-- either the same time or subsequently-- this action took like, six, eight days I think.
My mother and my sister Lola and my younger sister Frania were also in the selection line.

And my sister Lola tells me that, because I didn't see that for myself, was that she-- because they were young, my mother went to the left, which is the place to go to be deported, and my two sisters were to the right.
They were quite young.
And my younger sister, Frania, would not-- Lola was holding onto her hand and having her to stand with her.
She would not stand with her.
She you know, threw herself away, forced herself away, and ran towards the other side, where my mother was.
She would not be separated from my mother.
And even the Germans there said, you're young.
Yeah, don't-- don't go over.
You stay here.
You know, she's a very pretty young woman.
And you know, your mother, she's old.
You know, she can die.
But you should live.
You stay here.
But she wouldn't listen.
Not-- not really.
I did not-- I did not really understand that it was a matter of life and death.
No, I did not.
Yes, the grown ups knew.

Oh, they didn't want to know, but there were people that came into the ghetto and warned already, you know, that escaped from the trains, the locomotive engineers who drove the trains, and stuff like that, that told them.
So they-- they knew, but they didn't want to know.
But whatever they knew or not, it was one thing for absolutely certain-- they did not want to be deported.
So deportation meant something very bad, even if they did not know that it meant certain death at that time.
Again, I just attribute-- I don't think that they knew for sure.
They didn't want to know.
They didn't want to know.
No, no, that's all right.
But they certainly knew that the consequence of being deported was something very bad.
And it was much more preferable to stay.
Yeah, she-- she just forces herself to go to the other line, where mother was.
She was very, very close to mother.
And she wanted to be with her.
Whatever the consequences were, she wanted to be with her.
She didn't want to be separated from her mother.
The next thing that I remember is that my brother, my father, and I went to this little factory, this barrel making factory.
And there's names for it.
I can't-- I think it was the same day, but I cannot be certain.
Well subsequently, we know that what happened was that the German were liquidating the ghetto, that they were deporting-- at this time, the ghetto was probably holding close to 25,000 to 30,000 Jews.
Because a lot of those little villages like Soliev and the others-- the Jews were driven from there into Piotrkow, into the larger center.
And during this action, which took eight days, they would bring a train in.
And they would deport something like 6,000 people on the train, which was a cattle train.
And they would drive 150 people into one of these cattle cars.
They would be forced to leave all their possessions behind.

And, there was-- from what I understand, it was, you know, terrible scenes, because mothers, and fathers, and children-- and also the old people, the older people who-- there were some people in the ghetto who could not-- who could not physically walk to the assembly place.
They were paraplegic and they were people very sick in bed.
And the husbands wouldn't leave them, so the Gestapo and the Ukraine would shoot them.
They would shoot a lot of the old people right then and there.
They wouldn't even bother to put them on the train, because they would have more room to put others on the train.
I saw some of the killing, yeah, later on.
But I did not--
Yeah, but I did not see-- I did not see the train, no.
I was already gone.
No, no, this is-- what I'm saying here is historical facts that were told to me.
Yeah.
And we know from-- again, from history-- that they went to Treblinka.
And in Treblinka-- that was right outside Warsaw.
And that there was-- they were gassed.
OK, I remember being in that--
After the selection pro--
No, see, I have no recollection of my mother.
No.
No, I don't remember talking to her, no.
Uh-uh.
We went into this little factory.
And we were told to stay there for eight days.
Now, my father and my brother had these legal permits to work.
I was-- I did not.
As a youngster, I had no value to the Germans.
I had no legal standing to survive.
You know, that-- as a matter of fact, the children were the most vulnerable.
And so they had to hide me in that-- in that factory.
They hid me in some lofts.
And I was dirty and there were mice running around.
And all during the day, I had to stay hidden.
Because the danger was not so much the Germans, but it was that the Poles that worked there would inform on you.
You know.
So you know-- and I didn't-- being a young kid, I didn't like being hidden.
You know, I remember one time I ca-- you know, during the night, they would bring me down.
And I would be able to stay with them.
And I remember one day this German-- some police, German police, which they had these metal breasts around their chests-- some kind of-- there are so many different branches of them.
They came in.
We thought it would be-- the numbers would be up.
And they-- they were looking for a shoemaker.
They-- they knew-- he found one pair of boots, these long boots.
And he wanted to know what the other one was, or where the guy who could make him another pair.
So we go-- were really relieved with that.
You know, they just loved the quality of the shoes.
Now during-- also during that time, my brother tells the story that his-- some of his Polish friends that he used to pal around with, and drink, and play cards with-- they came into the place.
And they were working off of the Polish police.
And they told him to hand over any valuables that he has.
And they robbed him of anything that we had left they took.
And he turned to them.
He says, I thought you were my friends.
And they said, be glad that's all we're doing is just taking your possessions.
Because we could-- we could take you with us.
So that was a big disillusionment there.
You know, he thought they were his friends.
Before the war, they were.
No, we were stayed there.
We stayed there.
They were all back in the ghetto.
And while we were-- while we were in this factory, this deportation was taking place.
They only had one train each day.
And it was like, 5,000, 6,000 people packed on one train.
And you're talking about probably 20,000 people, something like that, having to be deported.
So this was going on while we were in this factory.
Probably about the whole duration of the Action, what the Germans called the Action.
It was, of course, eight days, we know.
After that, my memory kind of fails me, but it came back a little bit is that you know, we were all under jurisdiction of the SS, you know-- even the special branch of the whatever.
And we were-- this was not a labor camp as such.
It was just-- us be staying there overnight was just a temporary thing while the Action was taking place.
When the Action was over, we were told to go back to the ghetto.
Now the ghetto was shrunk now to just one block or so.
And now it was fenced in.
That I do remember.
It was fenced in with barbed wire and guarded, you see.
Now when we came back, most of the people were gone, were deported.
It was only like-- you know, again, there was negotiation.
The Jews always negotiated with the Germans-- for 2,000 legal people to stay behind to work in these factories, OK.
However, there were probably another 2,000 or so that were hidden in the cellars and in all their different hiding place.
Now this I remember very well.
This I have very clear memory of, is that the Jewish police and the German especially-- there was one German with a big German-- black German Shepherd.
And they would go.
And they would sniff out the hiding places.
And they would get the people up.
And they would-- lot of times they would shoot them.
I remember shooting them right there.
I remember I was standing, almost next to them, and the blood running on the ground, and all the brains coming out from their head.
The ones I remember was adults.
Then most of the-- then the Germans would tried all kinds of tricks to get the people out.
They said like, for example, we'll let you come out.
We'll make you legal.
And then, when they came out, they wouldn't.
And they took them to the great synagogue and interned them.
It was like 500 people at a time.
I have vague memories of that.
And I know that my sister, Lola, was-- was-- was caught.
And she was in one of those-- in that synagogue, or just before she got to a synagogue, some other place, she was able to escape.
And there were terrible things going on.
Like parents used to come to the synagogue.
And they would walk up.
They'd said, we'll trade places with our children.
Take us.
Or they would give them money.
And sometimes, some people did buy themselves out from there.
But anyhow, you know, I didn't see that obviously.
But from a historical point of view, the older people-- also there was terrible atrocities by the Ukrainians.
They would amuse themselves by taking babies and frying them in hot water and over fire and splitting their heads and shooting into the place.
I mean there was just-- there was no limits, no limits whatsoever.
But anyhow, the-- most of the people that were found hiding were eventually moved into the forest, outside of the town, very close.
They had to march there, dig their own graves, and they were all shot.
I don't know.
But it's all documented.
Maybe so.
We have a book.
I think maybe we should run a picture of it, because that's a historical facts of the whole town.
She has a very interesting story.
She has-- she was called for deportation.
And she was a housekeeper for a high German officer-- again, because she could speak German.
And he-- she went to him.
And he was there.
And he intervened.
And he was able to get her out.
And the second time, I think she literally escaped and was chased.
She literally escaped.
She had a lot of guts.
No, no, she stayed-- she stayed in the little ghetto.
